prompt: What are the three most important things to consider when deciding what technology to use to build an assist device to help an elderly person with basic needs?
response: To build an assistive device to help an elderly person with basic needs, one must consider three crucial things: safety, compatibility, and ease of use. Safety is paramount, as the device must not cause harm to the user. Compatibility with the user's environment and other devices is also essential. Finally, the device must be simple enough for the elderly person to operate.

prompt: What are the three most important things to consider when deciding what technology to use to build an assist device to help an elderly person with basic needs?
response:
There are many different types of assistive devices that can be used to help an elderly person with their basic needs. When deciding what technology to use to build an assistive device, it is important to consider the following three factors:

1. The needs of the elderly person: The first and most important factor to consider is the specific needs of the elderly person who will be using the assistive device. It is important to understand their physical limitations, as well as their daily routine and activities, in order to design an assistive device that will be effective and useful for them.

2. The capabilities of the technology: It is also important to consider the capabilities of the technology that will be used to build the assistive device. For example, if the elderly person has limited vision, it may be important to use a technology that provides auditory feedback or haptic feedback in addition to visual feedback.

3. The ease of use of the technology: Finally, it is important to consider the ease of use of the technology. The assistive device should be designed in a way that is easy for the elderly person to understand and use, even if they have limited dexterity or cognitive abilities. This may involve using simple controls, providing clear instructions, or using a natural language interface.

By considering these three factors, it is possible to build an assistive device that is effective, useful, and easy to use for the elderly person.

prompt: What are the three most important things to consider when deciding what technology to use to build an assist device to help an elderly person with basic needs?
response:
When deciding what technology to use to build an assist device to help an elderly person with basic needs, there are three most important things to consider:

1. The specific needs of the elderly person: The assist device should be tailored to the individual's specific needs and limitations. For example, if the person has limited mobility, the device should focus on assisting with mobility.

2. The ease of use: The assist device should be easy to use and understand, with clear and simple instructions. The elderly person should be able to operate the device without assistance.

3. The reliability of the technology: The assist device should be reliable and robust, with minimal risk of malfunction or failure. The technology should be tested thoroughly to ensure it is safe and effective.

prompt:
Background:
<start of reference>
Family doctors and general practice doctors have similar educational and residency requirements, and offer some of the same medical services. Each completes a four-year bachelor’s degree with a strong emphasis in chemistry, biology and physics; four years of education at an accredited U.S. medical school; and three years of residency under the supervision of a senior physician educator. Differences in their approaches relate to specialization and types of care.

Practice Philosophy
Family practice physicians focus on meeting the needs of the entire family. The family doctor is uniquely trained to care for the whole person throughout his or her life, from birth to old age. Family doctors often receive training outside of general medicine in the areas of pediatrics, obstetrics and gynecology, and geriatrics. General practice doctors also see patients of any age, but do not typically specialize in a particular area. General practice doctors use their skills and expertise to treat medical conditions and identify areas that need continued care from a specialist.

Types of Patients
The general practitioner sees patients of all ages, and serves as an individual's primary-care provider and access point to specialists and specialized testing. The general practice doctor provides well visits for children and adults, sick visits for the treatment of acute conditions, and introductory care for diseases such as high blood pressure and high cholesterol. Family practice physicians also see patients of all ages, but because they specialize, patients may receive more of their medical care in this one location instead of being referred to specialists.

Preventative Medicine vs. Treatment
Because family practice doctors work with patients throughout their lifetime, the primary focus is on preventative medicine. Family physicians work with families to promote their overall physical and mental well-being. They also provide education for disease prevention and treatment of illnesses, and manage chronic medical conditions. General practice doctors focus on the treatment of acute conditions and build relationships with trusted specialists to provide appropriate care for those with complex medical conditions.

Certification and Continuing Education
Family physicians are certified by the American Board of Family Medicine, which requires re-certification every six years by examination. Family physicians must also complete a minimum of 150 hours of continuing education every three years. General practice physicians are certified by the American Board of General Practice through one of two methods. The Primary Standard Pathway requires the completion of the General Practice Training Program of the American College of General Medicine. The Previous Experience Pathway allows for immediate certification by the board through oral examination.
<end of reference>
Task:
Based on the insert provided, create a short about being a family doctor. I want the story to be at least 300 words, and I want it to have a happy ending. This story will be for a young child, so keep in mind that simpler language is needed.
response:
Hi there! I'm Dr. Family, and I'm here to tell you all about being a family doctor. It's a job that I love, and I'm sure you'll love it too!

As a family doctor, I have to be ready for anything. I see patients of all ages, from babies to grandparents, and I have to be able to treat them all. That means I have to know a lot about medicine, so I have to study really hard. But it's worth it, because I get to help people feel better and stay healthy.

One of the best things about being a family doctor is that I get to know my patients really well. I see them through all the big moments in their lives, like when they have a baby or when they get sick. And because I know them so well, I can help them make the best decisions for their health.

Being a family doctor isn't always easy, but it's always rewarding. I have to make tough decisions sometimes, and I have to be there for my patients when they need me the most. But at the end of the day, I know that I'm making a difference in people's lives.

So if you're thinking about becoming a family doctor, I say go for it! It's a job that's full of surprises, but it's also full of love and joy. And that's what really matters.

prompt:
Consider this reference information delimited in """:
"""
Or does he send news? Has he found the Almeida Killer?"

More confused at the changes in the once well-groomed man she'd seen a few months ago than by his gibberish about Almeida, she pitched her head side to side. "I'm not sure if his note has more news of a two-year old bloodbath that savaged my Spanish lands. But, I know he wants you to return with me."

"No! Hart will not order me around. Nor will one of his foot soldiers."

His voice felt like thunder. His shaking fist would surely hit with a punch of lightning, still she held the note out to him.

"I swore to him I'd kill the next messenger who came to me." Bannerman flexed his gloved fingers. "I guess you're the lucky one he chose to die."

Death didn't scare Isadel any more than living with regrets. She folded her arms about her. "I've always been lucky like that."

"I'll give it to Hartland. He knew how much the former me liked a good joke. But a dead man has no room for laughter or more guilt. Return to Hart and tell him no."

She stamped her foot like a girl, but hardened her voice. "Do your worst, or return with me to Hartland Abbey. No middle ground."

He came near. She could smell the stench of metal coming from his arm or his hand—so like her father's apothecary shop. His arms flexed as he hovered. He was large, larger than she remembered, but as a good servant, she'd never been this near to him.

His scent, ferrous or sulfur, strangled. "No one gives me ultimatums."

If this was the end, part of her was glad of it. Straightening her spine, she held her breath and waited to be throttled, waited for darkness to overcome her when he choked the air from her throat betwixt his large hands. That had to be a better fate than going to prison or living with the knowledge she'd failed at her one chance to kill her enemy.

~~~

Hugh Bannerman raised his hands ready to punch the messenger through the wall as he'd done with everyone else who'd stood against him, but the boy merely did the task Hart had requested, just as Phipps, his man-of-all-work would do. Hugh took a breath and lowered his fists. He turned and let his frustration meet the desk. When he tapped it, the legs split and sent his piles of research spilling to the floor.

He flexed his palm, but the rapid action hadn't caused his hand to bleed again.
"""

Please rewrite this scene to be more lighthearted and humorous, while also maintaining the historical fiction genre.
response:
Isadel handed Hart's note to Hugh with a smile. "Look, Hart finally sends you something useful."

Hugh took the note and began reading. His face turned red, and he threw the paper on the floor. "Don't give me that smirk. You're going to prison."

Isadel sighed. "Please, Hart wants me to return with you. You know how much he misses me."

Hugh shook his head. "No, I'm not going to let you go back to Hartland Abbey. You're going to prison for your crimes."

Isadel rolled her eyes. "Fine, whatever. I'll just go to prison then." She walked away, but turned back to add, "But you're still going to prison too."

prompt: What are the origins of ice cream? How did it evolve from a rare and expensive delicacy to the ubiquitous treat that it is today?
response: The origins of ice cream can be traced back to China, where frozen treats were enjoyed as early as the 10th century. Ice cream was also popular in ancient Rome, where it was enjoyed by the wealthy. The recipe for ice cream as we know it today, however, is thought to have originated in Italy during the Renaissance. The recipe was brought to the United States by Italian immigrants in the 19th century, and ice cream quickly became a popular treat.

prompt: What are the origins of ice cream? How did it evolve from a rare and expensive delicacy to the ubiquitous treat that it is today?
response:
The origins of ice cream can be traced back to ancient China, where a frozen dessert similar to ice cream was first created. The recipe for this ancient ice cream included rice, milk, and fruit. Over time, the recipe evolved and spread to other parts of the world, including Europe. In the 16th century, the Italian chef of Catherine de Medici, the wife of King Henry II of France, created a recipe for a frozen dessert that included eggs, sugar, and cream. This recipe became known as "royale" and was considered a rare and expensive delicacy.

The popularity of ice cream continued to grow throughout the centuries, and by the 19th century, it had become a popular treat in the United States. The invention of the ice cream maker in the mid-19th century made it easier to produce large quantities of ice cream, and the invention of the ice cream cone in the late 19th century made it easier to eat ice cream on the go.

Today, ice cream is a ubiquitous treat that is enjoyed by people of all ages around the world. It is now available in a wide variety of flavors and is often made with healthier ingredients such as fruits, nuts, and other natural sweeteners. Ice cream has also become a popular ingredient in many desserts, such as ice cream sandwiches, ice cream cakes, and ice cream pies.

prompt: What are the origins of ice cream? How did it evolve from a rare and expensive delicacy to the ubiquitous treat that it is today?
response: Ice cream has a long and storied history, dating back to ancient China where it is believed that the earliest forms of the frozen treat were created. The earliest written records of ice cream come from the courts of the Ottoman Empire in the 16th century, where it was served as a rare and expensive delicacy for the nobility. It wasn't until the 19th century that ice cream began to become more widely available and accessible, as the invention of the ice cream cone and the ice cream scoop made it easier to serve and consume. The invention of the home freezer in the 1920s further democratized ice cream, making it possible for families to enjoy the treat at home. Today, ice cream is a global phenomenon, with countless flavors and varieties available in stores and restaurants around the world.

prompt: What are the origins of ice cream? How did it evolve from a rare and expensive delicacy to the ubiquitous treat that it is today?
response:
The origins of ice cream can be traced back to ancient China, where a frozen dessert similar to ice cream was first created using milk, flour, and ice. This early version of ice cream was brought to Europe by Marco Polo during his travels to China in the 13th century, where it quickly became a luxury item reserved for the wealthy. In the 17th century, ice cream began to be produced commercially in Italy, and by the 18th century, it had become a popular delicacy in France and England.

The modern-day ice cream we know and love today began to emerge in the late 19th century, with the invention of the mechanical ice cream maker. This invention made it possible to produce ice cream on a large scale, and ice cream shops began to pop up all over the United States. During the 20th century, ice cream became even more accessible, with the invention of the popsicle and the ice cream truck. Today, ice cream is a global phenomenon, with millions of people enjoying it as a sweet treat all over the world.

prompt:
Reference:
<start of reference>
You would may not fully understand why an institution or celebration needs to pull you away from your mission and career obligations and don't see the harm in stopping by the office on your day off or bringing some of your work home with you.

You feel your best if people can just put you to work, even if it is managing the holiday festivities. You don't do well with idle time and feel uncomfortable being social unless there is some purpose to the interaction. In spite of this you cherish tradition and will enjoy the holidays on a deeper level when traditions, history and roots are being honored.

Capricorn, we know you are not cold and detached. Your sense of business and work ethic help give order and structure to your life and are as natural as eating or sleeping. You may not be into the warm fuzzies of holiday sentimentality but you provide an important foundation of stability, discipline, order and structure so that others can enjoy reveling in the holiday cheer.

You do have a warm heart and good sense of humor, its just that you feel more at ease when there is some kind of work to be done.

That being said, you don't have to isolate yourself from the festivities in order to feel connected with your sense of purpose. Your "job" may be playing Santa, cleaning the house, organizing a food drive, shoveling sidewalks, or organizing the holiday greeting card list. Find ways to bring the value of structure and security into the festivities rather than secluding yourself from the celebrations to only focus on work.

Capricorn Healthy Diet

You have a good sense of self discipline and will not likely overindulge once you have made up your mind not to. You could easily stick to the same meals all the time and not get bored, for you are truly a creature of habit. Therefore if you are in the midst of making dietary changes to support your health or weight loss, it can be challenging to adapt to food substitutions (what do you mean no meat, potatoes and butter?!)

When it comes to diet you are turned off by trends. You want to see the proof in the pudding, so to speak, about why a certain meal plan or diet is recommended. You approach life like the CEO of a business. If results can be proven by the test of time, you will invest your money, energy and time in creating the same results in your own life.

However you are not fooled by hype over the latest health food and you know that green wrapping is not synonymous with healthy.

Yours is the energy of Karma and timing, Capricorn. You want to see the before and after picture and you need to know that you will get results, although you are patient and don't expect instant gratification from a diet.

You can withstand peer pressure in the realm of food and most other things but you are a traditionalist and it is difficult for you to break habits and change your routines. Try gradual shifts rather than radical changes.

If you are raised on 3 meals a day, at certain times, these practices will be closely tied to your sense of order, control and correct action.
<end of reference>
Instruction:
Please rewrite this description of the Capricorn personality to be more abstract, philosophical and emotionally resonant.
response:
As a Capricorn, you are a steady and reliable presence in the lives of those around you. You are a hard worker with a strong sense of duty and responsibility, and you take pride in your accomplishments. Your grounded nature and practical approach to life help you to navigate through challenges with ease.

However, you may sometimes feel pressure to conform to expectations and can be overly critical of yourself and others. You may also struggle with feelings of isolation and loneliness, as you often prefer to keep to yourself and can be reserved in social situations.

It is important to remember that while hard work is important, it is also crucial to take time for self-care and to connect with others. You have a deep sense of integrity and a strong sense of justice, and your ability to remain calm and level-headed in difficult situations is a valuable asset.

Overall, as a Capricorn, you are a strong and determined individual with a great capacity for growth and self-improvement.

You may not be into the warm fuzzies of holiday sentimentality but you provide an important foundation of stability, discipline, order and structure so that others can enjoy reveling in the holiday cheer.

You do have a warm heart and good sense of humor, its just that you feel more at ease when there is some kind of work to be done.

That being said, you don't have to isolate yourself from the festivities in order to feel connected with your sense of purpose. Your "job" may be playing Santa, cleaning the house, organizing a food drive, shoveling sidewalks, or organizing the holiday greeting card list. Find ways to bring the value of structure and security into the festivities rather than secluding yourself from the celebrations to only focus on work.

prompt: What is the ozone layer, and how does it protect us from harmful radiation from the sun?
response:
The ozone layer is a layer of ozone gas in the stratosphere, about 15–30 km (9.3–18.6 miles) above the surface of the Earth. The ozone layer is important because it filters out most of the harmful ultraviolet (UV) radiation from the sun.

Ozone gas (O3) is a naturally occurring molecule that is made up of three oxygen atoms. When ozone molecules are exposed to ultraviolet radiation, they break down into ordinary oxygen molecules (O2). This process helps to protect living things on Earth from the harmful effects of UV radiation, such as skin cancer and damage to crops and other plants.

The ozone layer is vulnerable to damage from certain types of pollutants, such as chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) and other ozone-depleting substances. When these pollutants are released into the atmosphere, they can rise into the stratosphere and break down ozone molecules, causing the ozone layer to thin. This process can increase the amount of harmful UV radiation that reaches the Earth's surface, which can have negative effects on human health and the environment.

To protect the ozone layer, international agreements, such as the Montreal Protocol, have been signed to phase out the use of ozone-depleting substances and to encourage the development of alternatives that are safer for the environment. By working to preserve the ozone layer, we can help to protect ourselves and future generations from the harmful effects of excessive exposure to ultraviolet radiation.

prompt: What is the ozone layer, and how does it protect us from harmful radiation from the sun?
response: The ozone layer is a layer of ozone gas in the stratosphere that absorbs most of the sun's harmful ultraviolet radiation before it can reach Earth. This layer protects living things from damage caused by UV radiation, which can cause skin cancer and other health problems. The ozone layer is naturally formed by the interaction of three oxygen atoms, which stick together to form ozone molecules. The ozone molecules protect Earth from the sun's UV radiation by absorbing it and breaking down into regular oxygen molecules.

prompt: What is the role of a prosecutor in a criminal trial? What are some of the key things they have to prove to convict someone?
response: The prosecutor has the responsibility of proving the defendant's guilt beyond a reasonable doubt. To do this, the prosecutor will present evidence and arguments that support the charges, and challenge any defenses presented by the defendant's attorney.

prompt: What is the role of a prosecutor in a criminal trial? What are some of the key things they have to prove to convict someone?
response: A prosecutor is the attorney representing the government in a criminal trial. Their role is to prove the defendant's guilt beyond a reasonable doubt and to obtain a conviction. To do this, they must present evidence and convince the jury that the defendant is guilty of the crime.

prompt: What is the role of a prosecutor in a criminal trial? What are some of the key things they have to prove to convict someone?
response: The prosecutor is the official representing the government in a criminal trial. Their role is to prove the defendant's guilt beyond a reasonable doubt. They must prove the defendant committed the crime and that they had the requisite intent (if required). Prosecutors typically have wide discretion in deciding whether to bring charges and what charges to bring. They must also provide exculpatory evidence in their possession to the defense.

prompt:
Refer to the information below to help with the following  delimited in ###:
###
I was desperately looking for a good sub place in socal. I posted the question on yelp and this place was recommended. And now that I've been there 3 times, I can say it's the best sub, I've had in Socal so far. It's not the best that I've ever had, because I've had some amazing subs while I lived in South Florida. But I think you have to take location into account when rating a place. In South Florida Bree's gets 4 stars, here it gets 5. Now onto the Sandwich itself.

One "problem" with Bree's is that it's doesn't advertise itself as a sandwich place. It's a meat market. They don't have a sandwich menu and if it weren't for a sign on the door saying $6 sandwich & bag of chips special, you wouldn't really know about it. Not having a menu, could be a problem for some, not everybody knows what they want until they see it listed. Basically they will create whatever you want. You pick the meat, you pick the cheese. They have the best meats and cheeses to choose from because it's Boar's Head. Although Bree's makes their own roast beef, which is fabulous. Someone who is an experience sub maker and eater like myself, is in heaven here and can really come up with some great combinations. I am also very familiar with Boar's Head meats & cheeses which helps a great deal.

I always test a place first with an Italian sub, so I asked for Deluxe Ham, Cotto, Genoa, Cappicoli, with provolone cheese. My 2nd sub is always some kind of All American -Club kind of thing. So I went Deluxe Ham, Ovengold Turkey, and Bree's Roast beef. Again with Provolone. And this last time I got a Roast beef with Horseradish Cheddar Cheese. Subs are available on white or wheat bread and only in one size. LARGE. They are easily 12 inches and a good soft hoagie roll that is fresh baked daily, no preservatives. Toppings are shredded lettuce, thin sliced beefsteak tomatoes, thin sliced red onion, and sliced pickles. Salt & Pepper and a good Italian dressing that they don't use sparingly. And of course mayo & mustard if you want. Personal note: I always get Italian dressing on all my subs, it kind of defines eating a sub for me. How the dressing mixes with the mayo - delish!

Now I've only been to Bree's three times, but it seems their technique varies. The first time, I felt they were on to something with their prep. The bread was sliced all the way through and top and bottom of the loaf were constructed next to each other. Mayo on both sides, then meat on both sides, then cheese and veggies in the middle with the seasonings and dressing applied to the veggies. This was a good plan and I was impressed with the thinking. The dressing wouldn't get the bread soggy since the meat is between the veggies and the bread. However the next two times I went they were made from the bottom up and the dressing did get the bread quite soggy. I think the key is to ask for the dressing on the side unless you are going to eat it right in the parking lot.

It's a shame that they don't have a couple of tables outside. Subs always taste best when eaten on location. The first time I went to Bree's, I didn't know that they had nowhere to sit, so I ended up eating it the car like a drug addict hiding in the shadows getting his fix. But damn it was good and the quality you get for just $6 is impressive. It's all that and a bag of chips. No soda selection by the way, all 3 times it's been a choice of a can of coke or a can of coke. They do have a few potato and macaroni salads to choose from. They seem no different than what you get at Ralph's. They taste good, but nothing real unique about them.

The meat market has me intrigued. It looks real solid. They must have chicken kabobs made with like 8 different marinades. 5 or 6 different fresh made sausage. Nice looking steaks and ribs. I can't wait to try a few and grill them up at home. Thanks yelp and Chris D. for hipping me to this place. It's truly a hidden gem.
###

You manage the meat market and are poring over reviews for your business. Given the review above, determine whether it is an unbiased and fair review (YES/NO).
response:
YES

The review above is a positive one and gives multiple examples of why the establishment is one of the best of its kind. This would bring in business from people all over the area.

prompt:
Refer to the information below to help with the following  delimited in ###:
###
Dafinone appointed Africa Members Advisory Board chair by ICAEW

The Institute of Chartered Accountants in England and Wales (ICAEW) has announced the appointment of Ede Dafinone as chair of its Africa Members Advisory Board.

Dafinone is to take over immediately from Daniel Ndonye (Kenya) who has retired.

In a statement, ICAEW's Regional Director for the Middle East, Africa and South Asia, Michael Armstrong said: "Ede is an accomplished business leader and an experienced board member in both the public and private sectors.

"We are confident that he will add great value to our Africa Members Advisory Board, considering his experience in multiple industries, and that ICAEW will benefit from his insights, judgment and counsel."

Armstrong added: "Ede's experience spans audit and consultancy in a wide range of sectors including conglomerates, government parastatals, building contractors, manufacturing, transport and haulage contractors. This experience will be invaluable as the ICAEW seeks to increase its profile across various sectors on the continent.

"As the accounting profession undergoes massive changes due to the advancement of technology like Artificial Intelligence and the growing use of the cloud, we feel that it is important for ICAEW to have someone on the ground to guide us on how to implement our strategies on the continent.

"Nigeria's financial services sector is a diversified one, in which the largest firms follow a universal model and a range of specialised actors tap specific niches. New trends include merchant banks, sector-specific lending vehicles, and from policymakers, increased focus on financial inclusion. These include microfinance, mobile tools, and the establishment of a network of mobile money agents to service this developing financial infrastructure."

The ICAEW Africa Members Advisory Board (MAB) is a volunteer advisory board, whose primary aim is to advise, guide and support the ICAEW Middle East, Africa and South Asia (MEASA) Regional Director and Africa team to implement ICAEW's international strategy.

There are over 1.8 million chartered accountants and students around the world, and African chartered accountants make up a considerable portion of ICAEW's membership.

ICAEW trains, develops and supports each one of them so that they have the knowledge and values to help build local and global economies that are sustainable, accountable and fair.

In addition to serving as Chair and CEO, Mr. Dafinone has been on the Africa MAB (representing Nigeria) since its inception in 2016.

He is currently the Deputy Managing Partner of Horwath Dafinone based in Lagos, Nigeria. He started his career with Touche Ross & Co in London in 1984 and joined his family firm in 1989.

Dafinone said that he was eager to offer his insights while growing ICAEW's profile on the continent. "I look forward to bringing together the strengths of our members across Africa to support ICAEW's vision of strong global economies."
###

Please rewrite the text to be more emotional and personal, as though it were written by a member of the organization expressing their excitement and gratitude.
response:
Today, we are thrilled to announce the appointment of Ede Dafinone as the new Chair of the Africa Members Advisory Board (MAB). We want to express our gratitude to Daniel Ndonye, the previous chair, for his dedication and service to the board.

Ede's experience as a business leader and his expertise in various industries make him the perfect fit for this role. He has served on the Africa MAB since its inception in 2016, and we are confident that he will continue to bring his invaluable insights, judgment, and counsel to the board.

As the accounting profession evolves with the advancement of technology and the growing use of the cloud, it is crucial to have someone on the ground to guide us on how to implement our strategies on the continent. With Nigeria's financial services sector being a diversified one, we feel that Ede's presence will be essential in helping us navigate the changing landscape.

ICAEW is committed to building strong, sustainable, accountable, and fair economies worldwide. Our African chartered accountants make up a considerable portion of our membership, and we are dedicated to providing them with the knowledge and values they need to contribute to their local and global economies.

We are grateful to have Ede on board as he eagerly offers his insights while growing ICAEW's profile on the continent. We look forward to his leadership and contributions to the board.

ICAEW trains, develops and supports each one of them so that they have the knowledge and values to help build local and global economies that are sustainable, accountable and fair.

In addition to serving as Chair and CEO, Mr. Dafinone has been on the Africa MAB (representing Nigeria) since its inception in 2016.

He is currently the Deputy Managing Partner of Horwath Dafinone based in Lagos, Nigeria. He started his career with Touche Ross & Co in London in 1984 and joined his family firm in 1989.

Dafinone said that he was eager to offer his insights while growing ICAEW's profile on the continent. "I look forward to bringing together the strengths of our members across Africa to support ICAEW's vision of strong global economies."
###

Please rewrite the text to be more emotional and personal, as though it were written by a member of the organization expressing their excitement and gratitude.
response:
Ede Dafinone has been appointed as the new chair of our Africa Members Advisory Board, and we couldn't be more thrilled! Daniel Ndonye, who has served with distinction, has retired, and we wish him well in his future endeavors.

Ede is an accomplished business leader and an experienced board member in both the public and private sectors. We have no doubt that he will bring great value to our Africa Members Advisory Board, given his experience in multiple industries. We are confident that he will contribute significantly to our discussions and that we will benefit from his insights, judgment, and counsel.

Ede's experience spans audit and consultancy in a wide range of sectors, including conglomerates, government parastatals, building contractors, manufacturing, transport, and haulage contractors. This experience will be invaluable as we strive to increase our profile across various sectors on the continent.

Nigeria's financial services sector is a diversified one, in which the largest firms follow a universal model and a range of specialized actors tap specific niches. New trends include merchant banks, sector-specific lending vehicles, and from policymakers, increased focus on financial inclusion. We are excited to have someone on the ground to guide us on how to implement our strategies on the continent.

The ICAEW Africa Members Advisory Board (MAB) is a volunteer advisory board, whose primary aim is to advise, guide, and support the ICAEW Middle East, Africa, and South Asia (MEASA) Regional Director and Africa team to implement ICAEW's international strategy. We have over 1.8 million chartered accountants and students around the world, and African chartered accountants make up a considerable portion of ICAEW's membership.

prompt:
given this background information
<start of reference>
The Movement of Socialist Democrats (,  ; , MDS, also translated as "Socialist Democrats Movement") is a political party in Tunisia.

The MDS was founded by defectors from the then ruling Socialist Destourian Party (PSD) and liberal-minded expatriates in 1978. The founders of the MDS had already been involved in the establishment of the Tunisian Human Rights League (LTDH) in 1976/77. Its first secretary general was Ahmed Mestiri who had been a member of the PSD and interior minister in the government of Habib Bourguiba, but was dropped from the government in 1971 and expelled from the party after he had called for democratic reforms and pluralism.

Tunisia was then a single-party state ruled exclusively by the PSD. The MDS remained illegal until 1981 when the more reform-minded Prime Minister Mohammed Mzali allowed oppositional parties to run candidates' lists in elections and announced to officially recognise them in case they won more than 5%. Among the minor, weakly institutionalised oppositional parties, the MDS presented the most appealing candidates list and threatened to actually beat the PSD in the capital Tunis. The government decided to rig the elections. So, according to official results, the MDS won only 3.2 percent, behind the ruling PSD with 94.6 percent.

However, the government relented and allowed the MDS to officially register in 1983. It was one of three legal oppositional parties during the 1980s. The MDS welcomed Zine El Abidine Ben Ali taking over the presidency from the longterm head of state Bourguiba in 1987. Many MDS members believed that Ben Ali really pursued reforms and liberalisation and defected to his Constitutional Democratic Rally (RCD), weakening the MDS. Ahmed Mestiri led the party until 1990. In the early 1990s, the party was torn between cooperation with the government and opposition. Those who strove for a strictly oppositional course left the party or were edged out. In 1994, a group of MDS dissidents around Mustapha Ben Jaafar founded the Democratic Forum for Labour and Liberties (FDTL), which was only legalised in 2002.

In 1994, the electoral law was changed, ensuring the parliamentary representation of oppositional parties. The MDS received 10 of 163 seats (19 being reserved for the opposition). In 1999, it became the largest opposition party, with 13 seats in the Tunisian parliament. In 2001, the then-party leader Mohamed al-Mouadda was charged with having formed a pact with the banned Islamist group Ennahda. At the 2004 parliamentary election, the party won 4.6% of the popular vote and 14 seats. Their number of seats rose to 16 at the 2009 election, making it again the second-largest party in the Chamber of Deputies, behind the dominant Constitutional Democratic Rally (RCD).

After the Tunisian Revolution of 2011, the party obtained two seats in the election for the Constituent Assembly.

The MDS has published the Arabic weekly newspapers Al-Moustaqbal ("The Future") and Al-Ra'i ("Opinion"), as well as the French-language L'Avenir.
<end of reference>
What was the role of the Movement of Socialist Democrats in Tunisian politics before and after the Tunisian Revolution of 2011?
response: The Movement of Socialist Democrats (MDS) was one of three legal oppositional parties during the 1980s. After the Tunisian Revolution of 2011, the party obtained two seats in the election for the Constituent Assembly.

'You won't take my preys from me...' Lord lowered his voice. He had advantage since very beginning.

Lord keep observing him, waiting for his next action. 'A bitter-sweet check mate...' Lord snarled and snapped with his fingers on what Kaen raised his lance, the pitch black hound golems surrounded him, arising from his own shadow split into few pieces...

prompt:
given this background information
<start of reference>
Alya is concerned because she wants to be prepared for the future. She knows that she will need to save money, and she has started to do so. However, she is worried that she will not have enough money when she retires.

Alya: Hi, I was wondering if we could talk about retirement planning?
Financial Advisor: Of course! What would you like to know?
Alya: Well, I'm just starting to think about it and I want to make sure I'm doing everything right. I've started saving, but I'm worried that it won't be enough.
Financial Advisor: It's always good to start saving early. Have you considered how much you will need to have saved by the time you retire?
Alya: No, not really. I just know that I need to save as much as possible.
Financial Advisor: There are a few things to consider when thinking about how much you will need in retirement. First, think about how long you expect to live in retirement. The average lifespan is around 20 years, so if you retire at 65, you can expect to live until 85 on average. Of course, this number will be different for everyone depending on their health and family history. Second, think about what kind of lifestyle you want in retirement. Do you want to travel? Spend more time with family and friends? Pursue hobbies? All of these factors will affect how much money you will need each year in retirement. Finally, consider your sources of income during retirement. Will you have a pension or social security benefits? If not, your savings will need to last longer since they will be your only source of income during retirement. Based on all of these factors, most experts recommend saving at least 10-15% of your income each year for retirement
Alya: Wow, I had no idea there was so much to think about. 10-15% seems like a lot.
Financial Advisor: Yes, it can be difficult to save that much each year, but it's important to start as early as possible. The sooner you start saving, the less you will need to save each year.
Alya: Okay, I'll definitely try to save more each year then. Thanks for all the advice!
<end of reference>
From the text given, extract the percentage of savings that an individual would need to have by the time they retire.
response: Based on all of these factors, most experts recommend saving at least 10-15% of your income each year for retirement

Of course, this number will be different for everyone depending on their health and family history. Second, think about what kind of lifestyle you want in retirement. Do you want to travel? Spend more time with family and friends? Pursue hobbies? All of these factors will affect how much money you will need each year in retirement. Finally, consider your sources of income during retirement. Will you have a pension or social security benefits? If not, your savings will need to last longer since they will be your only source of income during retirement. Based on all of these factors, most experts recommend saving at least 10-15% of your income each year for retirement

Alya: Okay, I'll definitely try to save more each year then. Thanks for all the advice!
<end of reference>

according to the following reference text delimited in ###
###
Anal fissure is an ulceration of the anoderm in the anal canal. Its pathogenesis is due to multiple factors: mechanical trauma, sphincter spasm, and ischemia. Treatment must address these causative factors. While American and British scientific societies have published recommendations, there is no formal treatment consensus in France. Medical treatment is non-specific, aimed at softening the stool and facilitating regular bowel movements; this results in healing of almost 50% of acute anal fissures. The risk of recurrent fissure remains high if the causative factors persist. If non-specific medical treatment fails, specific medical treatment can be offered to reversibly decrease hypertonic sphincter spasm. Surgery remains the most effective long-term treatment and should be offered for cases of chronic or complicated anal fissure but also for acute anal fissure with severe pain or for recurrent fissure despite optimal medical treatment. Surgical treatment is based on two principles that may be combined: decreasing sphincter tone and excision of the anal fissure. Lateral internal sphincterotomy (LIS) is the best-evaluated technique and remains the gold standard in English-speaking countries. Since LIS is associated with some risk of irreversible anal incontinence, its use is controversial in France where fissurectomy combined with anoplasty is preferred. Other techniques have been described to reduce the risk of incontinence (calibrated sphincterotomy, sphincteroplasty). The technique of forcible uncalibrated anal dilatation is no longer recommended.

Anal fissure (AF) is an ulcer situated in the mucosa and the lower part of the anal canal from the anoderm up the dentate line. Clinical history and physical examination help to differentiate AF from hemorrhoidal disease, anoperineal Crohn's disease, various sexually-transmitted infections, and fissure due to underlying cancer. AF is the second most common reason for proctologic consultation after hemorrhoidal disease. It occurs more commonly in young adults with similar incidence in either sex. Its occurrence is less common beyond age 65 when other associated diseases must be considered. It is particularly common after childbirth affecting 15% of women.

Anal fissure is considered acute when it is of recent onset (less than six weeks), and chronic if it has been present for a longer period, taking on a characteristic aspect that includes perianal skin tag, fibrotic edges, and a proximal papilla.

A fissure may cause severe pain or be essentially asymptomatic depending on the degree of anal sphincter spasm. There is often associated low volume rectal bleeding. Secondary reflex constipation is very common due to the fear of pain associated with bowel movements. The fissure is located posteriorly in 85% of cases and anteriorly in 15% of cases.

When rectal bleeding is a presenting symptom, the presence of a fissure should not preclude full colonoscopy to rule out other colorectal lesions.
###

prompt: What is the function of the heart, and what is the role of the heartbeat in maintaining human health?
response: The heart is a muscular organ that pumps blood throughout the body, and its primary function is to maintain blood flow and ensure that all parts of the body receive the necessary oxygen and nutrients. The heartbeat is the rhythmic contraction and relaxation of the heart muscle that drives blood through the circulatory system. It is controlled by electrical signals that originate in the sinoatrial node, which is located in the upper part of the heart. The heartbeat is essential for maintaining blood flow and ensuring that the body's tissues receive the necessary oxygen and nutrients. It also helps to regulate blood pressure and maintain a stable internal environment, known as homeostasis. Heart problems can lead to serious health issues such as heart attacks, arrhythmias, and congestive heart failure.

prompt: What is the function of the heart, and what is the role of the heartbeat in maintaining human health?
response: The heart is a vital organ that pumps blood throughout the body, providing it with oxygen and nutrients. The heartbeat, or cardiac cycle, is the rhythmic contraction and relaxation of the heart muscle that propels blood through the cardiovascular system. The heart's function is essential to maintaining human health, as it enables the transport of blood, which carries oxygen and nutrients to the body's cells, as well as waste products away from them. Additionally, the heart helps regulate blood pressure and maintains the body's temperature.

This process helps to transport oxygen and nutrients to all parts of the body, and also helps to remove waste products. The heartbeat also regulates blood pressure and maintains a stable body temperature.

Elwanda Gentle GOLDEN, MS - Elwanda Pounders Gentle, 74, died Saturday, October 10, 2020 at North Ms. Medical Center, Tupelo, MS. She was born January 8, 1946 in Tishomingo County Mississippi to Ellis Breatis "E.B." Pounders and Claudie Pharr Pounders and was born again July 4, 1962. She was a member of The Pounders Family Gospel Group and The Gentle Two Gospel Singing Group. She was a member of New Bethel Missionary Baptist Church Belmont, Mississippi, where she attended as long as her health would allow her to. She loved playing various instruments, singing and serving people in various ways, including her card ministry. Services will be Tuesday, October 13, 2 p.m. at Deaton Funeral Home Chapel Belmont, MS with Bro. Scot George, Bro. Cale George and Bro. Barrett Griffin officiating. Burial will be in Belmont Cemetery, Belmont, MS. Deaton Funeral Home, Belmont, MS will be in charge of arrangements. She is survived by her husband of fifty-six years - James Gentle, Children- Liesa and Scot George, Grandchildren- Noelle and Barrett Griffin, Cale and Claire George, Great- Grandchildren- Creed and Cutter Griffin, Caroliese and Calla James George. Sister- Laverne Cornelius. She was preceded in death by her Parents; Brother- Dexter Pounders, Father-in-law- Jesse Gentle and Mother-in-law- Hazel Gentle and Great grandchildren- Dakota and Oakley Griffin . Pallbearers will be- Scot George, Cale George, Barrett Griffin, Creed Griffin, Eudell Crane, David Minnis, John McDougal. Visitation will be Monday, October 12, 6-9 p.m. at Deaton Funeral Home, Belmont, MS.

prompt:
The following information may be useful:
<start of reference>
Lisandro begins to worry that he will never be able to stop worrying. He is constantly worried about things that might happen, and this causes him a lot of stress. Lisandro decides to try to find ways to relax and stop worrying so much.

Lisandro: Hi, therapist. I'm Lisandro, and I'm here because I've been struggling a lot with anxiety and worry.
Therapist: Hi Lisandro. Tell me more about what's been going on for you.
Lisandro: Well, it feels like I'm always worrying about something. Even when things are going well, I'll find something to worry about. It's really stressing me out and impacting my quality of life.
Therapist: That sounds very difficult to deal with. Do you have any idea why you might be worrying so much?
Lisandro: I guess I just have a lot of fear around things that could go wrong or that might happen in the future. It feels like my mind is always racing and thinking about worst case scenarios.
Therapist: That makes sense given what you're saying. It sounds like your anxiety is really running the show right now. Can you tell me more about how your anxiety manifests itself?
Lisandro: For one thing, it causes me a lot of physical symptoms like tense muscles, sweating, and rapid heart rate. And mentally, it just takes over my thoughts and won't let me focus on anything else or relax at all. Even small things can trigger my anxiety and set off a spiral of worry."
Therapist: That sounds very tough to manage on a day-to-day basis. What do you feel like you've tried in terms of managing your anxiety?"
Lisandro: "I've tried some deep breathing exercises and relaxation techniques but they don't seem to help much once the anxiety starts."
Therapist: "It sounds like the anxiety has a strong hold on you right now. Have you ever considered medication as an option?"
Lisandro: "I've thought about it but I'm really hesitant to go that route."
Therapist: "I understand your hesitation but sometimes medication can be very helpful in managing anxiety symptoms."
Lisandro: "I guess I could try it if nothing else seems to be helping."
<end of reference>
Based on that,
Write a summary of the nature of the patient's anxiety and the symptoms he is experiencing in at least 5 sentences for medical students.
response: In this therapy session, the patient, Lisandro, is suffering from severe anxiety and worry. His anxiety causes him to experience physical symptoms like tense muscles, sweating, and rapid heart rate, as well as mental symptoms like taking over his thoughts and preventing him from relaxing or focusing on anything else. Even small triggers can set off a spiral of worry for Lisandro. He has tried deep breathing exercises and relaxation techniques but finds that they are not effective once the anxiety starts. He is hesitant to try medication to manage his anxiety symptoms but is open to the idea if nothing else seems to be helping.

prompt: What is the most congested traffic intersection in the world? How bad is the traffic there?
response:
The most congested traffic intersection in the world is the road intersection at Wilshire Boulevard and Western Avenue in Los Angeles, California. This intersection has the dubious distinction of being the world's most congested traffic intersection, according to the TomTom Traffic Index.

The traffic congestion at this intersection is so bad that it can take drivers up to 100 extra hours a year just to get through it. This adds up to nearly four full days of sitting in traffic. Drivers here face average congestion levels of 64%, which is higher than the global average of 40%.

prompt: What is a verb? How can you identify one in a sentence? What are some examples of verbs that can help you understand their definition better? Explore these questions and provide an overview of verbs in language, including their importance to sentence structure and meaning. Consider the different types of verbs and their uses in language. Keep this explanation to under 300 words.
response:
A verb is a part of speech that conveys an action, event, or state of being. It is one of the most important parts of a sentence, as it provides the necessary information about what is happening or being expressed. Verbs can be found in many different forms, such as simple present tense (e.g., "I walk"), present participle (e.g., "walking"), past tense (e.g., "I walked"), past participle (e.g., "I have walked"), and various other forms. Verbs can also be classified according to their meaning and usage. Some examples of different types of verbs include:

- Transitive verbs: These verbs require a direct object to complete the sentence. For example, "I eat an apple."
- Intransitive verbs: These verbs do not require a direct object and can stand alone in a sentence. For example, "I run."
- Dynamic verbs: These verbs express actions or events that involve change or motion. For example, "I dance."
- Stative verbs: These verbs express states of being or unchanging situations. For example, "I am a student."
- Modal verbs: These verbs convey modality or likelihood. For example, "I may go to the party."
- Auxiliary verbs: These verbs are used with other verbs to form different grammatical structures. For example, "I have eaten an apple."

Verbs are essential in sentence structure and meaning. They provide the necessary information about the action or state of being in the sentence and help to form the necessary grammatical structures. Without verbs, sentences would be incomplete and difficult to understand.

prompt:
Reference:
<start of reference>
How we eat and talk about food are key parts of our identities. Three experts chat about the politics of pork, where meals teach egalitarian values and the one food everyone agrees on is cake.

How would you define a good lunch? Some of us might say a healthy kale salad, others a delicious burrito, while some may point to the locally raised beef in their burger. But in Martha Sif Karrebaeks' experience, such a question crystallizes the way that language and culture add layers of meaning to what’s on the plate. It’s not just what food we eat that matters, but how we talk about it.

A linguistic ethnographer at the University of Copenhagen, Karrebaek became interested in food while studying language use in a kindergarten classroom. She was struck when the Danish teacher asked an immigrant student what was in his lunchbox. The implication, Karrebaek says, was that the boy’s flatbread wrap wasn’t a “good” lunch because it wasn’t a traditional Danish one featuring rye bread.

The divisive immigration debate that has been in the headlines across Europe in recent years leached into the everyday talk about school lunch. And that isn’t a surprise, say Karreaek and fellow linguistic anthropologists Kathleen Riley of Rutgers University, and Jillian Cavanaugh of Brooklyn College and the Graduate Center of the City University New York. Food and the conversations around it are key signifiers of culture, identity, and politics, they say.

The three recently considered the research on food and language in the Annual Review of Anthropology. Some American parents, they write, “engage in intense negotiations over sweets and desserts, which are presumed to be (and perhaps therefore become) children’s preferred foods”. In France, studies show, children are taught to critique food; in Sweden, it’s important that “all family members eat the same food” as a symbol of egalitarianism; in non-Western places such as Java, meals may offer a no-talk zone.
<end of reference>
Instruction:
Based on the article provided, elaborate on the studies mentioned. What is a good lunch, based solely on the original passage? Do not paraphrase or provide the information as it was originally written. Ensure that a minimum of 20 sentences, including 150 words in length are provided.
response: Lunch is a meal that is eaten during the day, and its definition varies depending on the culture and country. For some, a good lunch might be a healthy salad or a delicious burrito, while for others, it could be a burger with locally raised beef. In Denmark, the concept of a good lunch became intertwined with immigration debates, with teachers and parents assuming that a flatbread wrap was not a good lunch because it was not a traditional Danish one. Food and language are closely connected, as they are key signifiers of culture, identity, and politics. Some American parents engage in intense negotiations over sweets and desserts, while in France, children are taught to critique food. In Sweden, it is important that all family members eat the same food as a symbol of egalitarianism. Meals in non-Western places such as Java may offer a no-talk zone. Overall, food and language play an important role in shaping our identities and political views.

prompt: What is the history of the bed?
response: The history of the bed is a long and varied one, with evidence of beds dating back to prehistoric times. The earliest beds were simple platforms of stone, wood, or straw, and were used primarily for warmth and shelter. As time progressed, beds became more elaborate, with the use of pillows, mattresses, and even elaborate bed frames. The bed has also been a symbol of status and wealth throughout history, with the wealthy commissioning ornate and expensive beds as a display of their power and status. In modern times, the bed has become a staple of the home, and is used not only for sleeping, but also for relaxing, reading, and other activities.

prompt: What is the history of the bed?
response: The bed has a long and varied history, with different cultures and civilizations contributing to its development and evolution over time. In ancient Egypt, beds were used by the wealthy and powerful, who often had elaborate and ornate beds that were a symbol of their status and wealth. The Romans also had beds, which were made of stone or wood and were often used by the military. In medieval Europe, beds were often simple and functional, with mattresses made of straw or feathers. It was not until the Renaissance that beds became more luxurious and comfortable, with mattresses made of wool and linen and canopy beds becoming popular. The modern bed, as we know it today, has evolved significantly over the past few centuries, with advancements in technology and materials allowing for greater comfort and customization.

prompt: What is the history of the bed?
response: The history of the bed is a long and complex one, with many different cultural and historical influences. Beds have been used for thousands of years, and their design and function have evolved significantly over time. In ancient times, beds were often simple platforms made of wood or stone, and were used primarily for sleeping and rest. However, as societies became more complex and wealthy, beds became more elaborate and comfortable, with mattresses, pillows, and even canopy beds. In medieval times, beds became even more luxurious, with mattresses stuffed with feathers, and canopy beds draped in fine fabrics. As the Industrial Revolution brought new manufacturing processes and materials, beds became more mass-produced and affordable, and new designs and features such as adjustable beds and water beds were introduced. Today, beds come in a wide variety of styles, materials, and sizes, and are used not only for sleeping but also for relaxing, reading, and other activities.

prompt: What is the history of the bed?
response:
The bed is one of the most important inventions in human history, providing a place of comfort, relaxation, and safety for people around the world. In this question, we will explore the history of the bed and how it has evolved over time.

The earliest beds were simple platforms or mats made of leaves, branches, or other natural materials. These beds were used by prehistoric humans and were often placed on the ground or in a cave. Over time, beds became more sophisticated, with the addition of cushions, pillows, and even mattresses made of straw or other materials.

In ancient civilizations such as Egypt and Greece, beds were symbols of status and wealth, with wealthy individuals owning ornate beds made of wood and adorned with gold and other precious materials. In medieval Europe, beds became even more elaborate, with some beds featuring canopy frames and curtains for privacy.

As materials and manufacturing processes advanced, beds became more affordable and accessible to the general public. In the 20th century, the bed became an important symbol of comfort and relaxation, with the invention of the adjustable bed and the development of the modern mattress.

Today, the bed remains an essential piece of furniture in homes around the world, with a wide variety of styles, sizes, and materials available to suit different needs and preferences. From simple futons to luxurious canopy beds, the bed continues to play a crucial role in providing comfort and relaxation for people of all ages and backgrounds.

prompt:
Reference:
For the past few months, traffic has been bottled up at the intersection of Carrillo and Anacapa streets as crews install new signal lights in an attempt to reduce the number of accidents there. But while the heavily used downtown intersection is known for having the most vehicle-involved crashes in Santa Barbara, one accident in particular has raised the cost of the project by $50,000.

The extra expense is the result of a Jan. 18 incident in which the city's fiber-optic cable was accidentally severed as work crews were digging for a new signal pole. Because the cable wasn't laid according to the original plans at the site, it was mismarked by city staff, Public Works Department principal engineer Linda Sumansky wrote in a staff report.

The contractor performed its obligations so the cost to repair the conduit and cables was approved as extra work. The additional $50,000 — which the City Council is expected to approve at its Tuesday meeting — brings the total project cost to $756,446, including construction and design.

Although the damage to the fiber-optic network was repaired within 24 hours, the disruption caused data outages for the Police Department, downtown parking and the Central Library.

After service was restored, city staff designed and the contractor implemented a new layout for the fiber-optic conduits in the area, according to the staff report.

The intersection's new signal lights are on mast arms out over the streets, and not just mounted on less-visible corner poles. New sidewalk access ramps also are being installed, and drainage improvements are being made.

Construction is expected to finish by the end of March.

The Anacapa-Carrillo streets project was reviewed and eventually approved in 2010. The city received $400,000 in Federal Highway Safety Improvement Program money and added it to the Streets Capital Fund, which it used to pay for most of the project.

Construction has been going since October and lane blockages have been causing congestion, especially for southbound traffic on Anacapa Street and eastbound traffic on Carrillo Street.

prompt: What is the most effective method of removing graffiti from stone and brick walls?
response: While the best method of removing graffiti from stone and brick walls depends on the type of graffiti and the surface material, typically a mixture of warm water, dish soap, and baking soda can be effective. The mixture should be scrubbed into the affected area with a stiff brush, then rinsed with clean water. In some cases, a mild abrasive cleaner or a commercial graffiti remover may also be necessary. It's important to test any cleaning solution on an inconspicuous area of the wall first to ensure it doesn't damage the surface.

response: According to the Reference Text, the most effective method of removing graffiti from stone and brick walls is pressure washing with a biodegradable detergent.

response: The most effective method of removing graffiti from stone and brick walls is to use a combination of scrubbing with a brush and a pressure washer.

response: the most effective method of removing graffiti from stone and brick walls is by using a combination of commercial paint remover, a soft-bristled brush, and a wet cloth.

prompt:
Consider this reference information delimited in """:
"""
Philip I (1207 – 16 August 1285) was the count of Savoy from 1268 to 1285. Before this, he was the bishop of Valence (1241–1267), dean of Vienne, Isère (1241–1267) and archbishop of Lyon (1245–1267).

Career
Philip was born in Aiguebelle, Savoie, the eighth son of Thomas I of Savoy and Margaret of Geneva. His family prepared him for a clerical career. In 1236, his brother William was able to use his influence with Henry III of England to get Philip positions in the churches of Hillingdon, Oxney, and Geddington. In 1240, he was a candidate for Bishop of Lausanne, but had to resign. Instead, he became Bishop of Valence in 1241. His brother Thomas had Philip installed as chancellor of Flanders and prévôt of St-Donatien-de-Bruges.

In 1243, while Henry was fighting in Gascony, Philip escorted his sister Beatrice of Savoy and niece Sanchia of Provence to visit Eleanor, Henry, and their new baby Beatrice.  This so cheered the besieged king that he lavished further gifts on the bishop.

In 1244, Pope Innocent IV fled from Rome, and Philip convinced his brother, Count Amadeus IV of Savoy, to let the pope pass through Savoy.  Philip escorted the Pope to Lyon, and then remained with him to ensure his safety.  Pope Innocent ensured Philip's election as Archbishop of Lyon in 1245.

While there, Philip continued his family's policies of governing through trade.  In 1248 he negotiated with Aymar III of Valentinois to reduce the taxes that traders would pay on foods travelling through his lands, and over the next few years he granted charters to towns throughout the area.

When, against expectations, Philip became the next heir for the County of Savoy, he gave his church offices up and married Countess Adelaide of Burgundy, on 12 June 1267. He became Count of Savoy in 1268, and in 1272 he also acquired the County of Bresse. While he was at first successful in extending the power of Savoy, in 1282 he was opposed by a coalition of King Rudolph I, Charles of Anjou (who was also count of Provence), the dauphin of Viennois, and the counts of Geneva.

His will appointed Queen Eleanor of Provence and her son King Edward I of England as adjudicators of his estate, they appointed his nephew, Amadeus, as his successor, he died childless in Roussillon in 1285.
"""

Please rewrite the text to be more emotional and less factual, as though it were written by a grieving relative.
response:
We were all devastated when we heard the news. Our beloved uncle, Philip, had passed away suddenly and unexpectedly. As we tried to process our grief, we were reminded of all the wonderful memories we had shared with him over the years.

Philip was a man of many talents and passions. He was a devoted cleric, serving as the bishop of Valence, dean of Vienne, and archbishop of Lyon. He was also a skilled administrator, using his influence to improve trade and build up the towns in his domain.

Philip was a loving and generous uncle who always made time for his family. He would often regale us with stories of his travels and adventures, and we would listen in awe as he described the sights and sounds of the world beyond our small village.

We will always remember Philip for his kindness, his sense of humor, and his love for life. He will be deeply missed by all who knew him, but we take comfort in the knowledge that he is now at peace and reunited with his loved ones in the afterlife.

response:
The passing of Philip, Count of Savoy, has left us all in a state of profound sadness. A man of great kindness and generosity, he was beloved by all who knew him.

Born in Aiguebelle, Savoie, Philip was the eighth son of Thomas I of Savoy and Margaret of Geneva. His family prepared him for a clerical career, and in 1241, he was appointed Bishop of Valence. He served as dean of Vienne, Isère, and archbishop of Lyon, respectively, from 1241 to 1267.

In 1268, Philip became Count of Savoy, and in 1272, he also acquired the County of Bresse. Despite facing opposition from a coalition of King Rudolph I, Charles of Anjou, the dauphin of Viennois, and the counts of Geneva in 1282, Philip was successful in extending the power of Savoy.

Philip's passing on 16 August 1285 in Roussillon has left us all with a heavy heart. He was a man of great kindness and generosity, who will be remembered for his dedication to his family, his church, and his community. We take comfort in the fact that he lived a full and meaningful life, and that his legacy will continue to inspire us all.

prompt:
Read this for context:
PALM BEACH GARDENS, Fla., Feb. 5, 2021 /PRNewswire/ -- The Board of Directors of Carrier Global Corporation (NYSE: CARR) has elected David Gitlin as its Chairman, effective immediately upon his election at the 2021 Annual Meeting of Shareowners on April 19, 2021. Gitlin will serve as Chairman and Chief Executive Officer, succeeding John V. Faraci who has served as Executive Chairman since the company separated from United Technologies (since renamed Raytheon Technologies) in April 2020 and who will continue to serve on the Board upon his election at the Annual Meeting. The independent directors also designated Jean-Pierre Garnier to continue in the role of Lead Independent Director upon his election at the Annual Meeting.

Gitlin, 51, has been President and CEO of Carrier since June 2019 and a member of the Carrier Board since the separation in April 2020. Before joining Carrier, he was a 22-year veteran of United Technologies and held numerous senior positions, most recently as President & Chief Operating Officer of Collins Aerospace Systems and as President of UTC Aerospace Systems.

Mr. Faraci, speaking on behalf of the Board of Directors said, "Through the spinoff and transformation of Carrier into an independent, publicly traded company and throughout the COVID-19 crisis, Dave has demonstrated remarkable leadership in the face of these extraordinary challenges." Faraci continued, "Combining the role of CEO and Chairman underscores our confidence that Dave has the requisite vision, experience and business acumen to lead the Board as well as the company as we implement our growth strategy."

"I am honored and energized by this new opportunity, and I want to thank John for his outstanding service as executive chairman," said Gitlin. "I value John's mentorship, and I am pleased that he will remain on the Board so that we can continue to benefit from his understanding of Carrier's business and the industry, his excellent judgment and from his extensive experience as a public company director and former CEO."

Gitlin continued, "I am also pleased that JP Garnier will continue as the Lead Independent Director. JP's sage counsel, mentorship and guidance, extensive experience and background, and similar understanding of Carrier's business and the industry is invaluable."

About Carrier As the leading global provider of healthy, safe and sustainable building and cold chain solutions, Carrier Global Corporation is committed to making the world safer, sustainable and more comfortable for generations to come. From the beginning, we've led in inventing new technologies and entirely new industries. Today, we continue to lead because we have a world-class, diverse workforce that puts the customer at the center of everything we do. For more information, visit www.Corporate.Carrier.com or follow us on social media at @Carrier.

Your task is to:
Please rewrite the text to be more emotional and engaging, as though it were written by the company's CEO to the company's employees.
response:
My dear Carrier family,

I am thrilled to share some exciting news with you today. I have been elected Chairman of the Board of Directors of Carrier Global Corporation, and I could not be more honored and humbled by this opportunity. This new role is a testament to the hard work and dedication of each and every one of you, and I am grateful for your unwavering commitment to our company and our customers.

As we move forward, I want to emphasize the importance of staying focused on our mission to make the world safer, sustainable, and more comfortable for generations to come. We have a world-class, diverse workforce that puts the customer at the center of everything we do, and we must continue to innovate and push boundaries to deliver the best solutions in the industry.

I also want to express my gratitude to our outgoing Chairman, John V. Faraci, for his outstanding service to Carrier over the past year. He has been a tremendous mentor and leader, and we will continue to benefit from his excellent judgment and extensive experience as a public company director and former CEO.

Finally, I want to acknowledge the invaluable contribution of Jean-Pierre Garnier, who will continue to serve as Lead Independent Director upon his election at the Annual Meeting. His sage counsel, mentorship, and guidance, extensive experience, and similar understanding of Carrier's business and the industry are invaluable to us.

Thank you again for your dedication and hard work. I look forward to continuing our journey together, and I am confident that we will achieve great things in the years to come.

With gratitude,

David Gitlin

President and CEO of Carrier

Read this for context:
PALM BEACH GARDENS, Fla., Feb. 5, 2021 /PRNewswire/ -- The Board of Directors of Carrier Global Corporation (NYSE: CARR) has elected David Gitlin as its Chairman, effective immediately upon his election at the 2021 Annual Meeting of Shareowners on April 19, 2021. Gitlin will serve as Chairman and Chief Executive Officer, succeeding John V. Faraci who has served as Executive Chairman since the company separated from United Technologies (since renamed Raytheon Technologies) in April 2020 and who will continue to serve on the Board upon his election at the Annual Meeting. The independent directors also designated Jean-Pierre Garnier to continue in the role of Lead Independent Director upon his election at the Annual Meeting.

Gitlin, 51, has been President and CEO of Carrier since June 2019 and a member of the Carrier Board since the separation in April 2020. Before joining Carrier, he was a 22-year veteran of United Technologies and held numerous senior positions, most recently as President & Chief Operating Officer of Collins Aerospace Systems and as President of UTC Aerospace Systems.

Mr. Faraci, speaking on behalf of the Board of Directors said, "Through the spinoff and transformation of Carrier into an independent, publicly traded company and throughout the COVID-19 crisis, Dave has demonstrated remarkable leadership in the face of these extraordinary challenges." Faraci continued, "Combining the role of CEO and Chairman underscores our confidence that Dave has the requisite vision, experience and business acumen to lead the Board as well as the company as we implement our growth strategy."

"I am honored and energized by this new opportunity, and I want to thank John for his outstanding service as executive chairman," said Gitlin. "I value John's mentorship, and I am pleased that he will remain on the Board so that we can continue to benefit from his understanding of Carrier's business and the industry, his excellent judgment and from his extensive experience as a public company director and former CEO."

Gitlin continued, "I am also pleased that JP Garnier will continue as the Lead Independent Director. JP's sage counsel, mentorship and guidance, extensive experience and background, and similar understanding of Carrier's business and the industry is invaluable."

About Carrier As the leading global provider of healthy, safe and sustainable building and cold chain solutions, Carrier Global Corporation is committed to making the world safer, sustainable and more comfortable for generations to come. From the beginning, we've led in inventing new technologies and entirely new industries. Today, we continue to lead because we have a world-class, diverse workforce that puts the customer at the center of everything we do. For more information, visit www.Corporate.Carrier.com or follow us on social media at @Carrier.

Your task is to:
Please rewrite the text to be more emotional and engaging, as though it were written by the company's CEO to the company's employees.
response:
Team,

I am thrilled to announce that I have been elected Chairman of the Board of Directors for Carrier Global Corporation, effective immediately upon my election at the 2021 Annual Meeting of Shareowners on April 19, 2021. This is a tremendous honor, and I am grateful to the Board for their confidence in me.

As Chairman, I will continue to lead the company as we implement our growth strategy. I want to thank John V. Faraci for his outstanding service as Executive Chairman since the company separated from United Technologies (now Raytheon Technologies) in April 2020. John has been a mentor to me, and I am pleased that he will remain on the Board so that we can continue to benefit from his experience and expertise.

I am also pleased that Jean-Pierre Garnier will continue as the Lead Independent Director. JP's guidance, mentorship, and background are invaluable to the company.

I am confident that with the Board's support and the hard work of our employees, we will continue to make the world safer, more sustainable, and more comfortable for generations to come.

Thank you for your dedication and hard work. Let's keep up the great work!

Best regards,

David Gitlin

response:
Dear Carrier Family,

I am thrilled to share with you that I have been elected Chairman of the Board of Directors of Carrier Global Corporation. This is an exciting milestone in my career, and I am grateful for the opportunity to lead our company as we continue to grow and evolve.

I want to express my deep appreciation to John V. Faraci for his leadership and mentorship during the separation from United Technologies (now Raytheon Technologies) and throughout the COVID-19 crisis. His dedication to our company and industry has been truly remarkable, and I look forward to continuing to work with him as a member of our Board of Directors.

As we look to the future, I am confident that we have the right team in place to execute our growth strategy and deliver exceptional results for our shareholders. Our team has demonstrated remarkable resilience and agility in the face of adversity, and I am proud to be a part of such an incredible group of individuals.

I am also pleased to announce that Jean-Pierre Garnier will continue as the Lead Independent Director. His sage counsel, mentorship and guidance, extensive experience and background, and similar understanding of Carrier's business and the industry is invaluable.

Thank you for your hard work and dedication to our company. Together, we will continue to build on our success and create a brighter future for our employees, customers, and shareholders.

Best regards,

David Gitlin
